# From linear to nonlinear mean

- So far our models are [linear]{.alert}
  - Ordinary least squares
    - Model: $Y \sim \Norm(\mu(\vx), \sigma^2)$, $\mu(\vx) = \vx^\top \vbeta$
    - Model with data: $\vY \sim \Norm(\mX\vbeta, \sigma^2 \mI)$
  - One-way ANOVA: categorical predictors, still linear mean
    - Model: $Y \sim \Norm(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ for group $i = 1, \ldots, k$
    - Model with data: $Y_{ij} \sim \Norm(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ independent, where $i = 1, \ldots, k$ and $j = 1, \ldots, n_i$

- What if
  - $Y \in \{0,1\}$ (success/failure) [Bernoulli]{.alert} random variable with explanatory variables $\vx$?
    - $\Ex[Y \mid \vx] = p(\vx)$ with $p(\vx) \in [0,1]$
  - But $p(\vx) = \vx^\top \vbeta$ can fall outside $[0,1]$
    - Need a [nonlinear]{.alert} function of $\vx$ to model $p(\vx)$

## Binary response setup

- Explanatory variables, $\vx \in \reals^d$ like OLS
- Response data: $Y_i \in \{0,1\}$

- Define $p_i = \Prob(Y_i = 1 \mid \vx_i)$, so $Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)$

- Need to model $p_i$ with a [nonlinear]{.alert} transformation that maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$


### Logistic function

  $$
  p_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}} \qquad \text{or equivalently} \qquad \log (\text{odds}) = \logit(p_i) = \log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
  $$

- $\beta_j = \text{change in log-odds per unit increase in } x_j$
- $e^{\beta_j} = \text{multiplicative change in odds per unit increase in } x_j$
- $\logit$ is the quantile function of the logistic distribution
  - which has CDF $\displaystyle F(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-t}}$, $- \infty < t < \infty$


## Likelihood

- Bernoulli likelihood:
  $$
  L\bigl(\vbeta \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)
  =
  \prod_{i=1}^n
  p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i} = \prod_{i=1}^n
  \left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)^{y_i}
  \left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)^{1-y_i}
  $$

- Log-likelihood:
  \begin{align*}
  \ell\bigl(\vbeta \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)
  & =
  \sum_{i=1}^n
  \left[
  y_i \log(p_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i) \right] \\
  & = \sum_{i=1}^n
  \left[
  y_i \log\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right) + (1-y_i)\log\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{\vx_i^\top \vbeta}}\right)
  \right]
  \end{align*}

- Estimate $\vbeta$ by MLE $\hvbeta = \argmax_\vb \ell\bigl(\vb \mid \{(\vx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n\bigr)$
  - No closed form solution
  - Use numerical optimization (e.g., Newtonâ€“Raphson / IRLS)

## Estimation by iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS)

- MLE: $\hvbeta = \arg\max_{\vb} \ell(\vb)$

- Solve: $\nabla \ell(\vbeta)=0$ (no closed form)

- Iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS):

  - Initialize $\vbeta^{(0)} = \vzero$

  - For $t = 0,1,2,\ldots$ until convergence:

    - $\vp^{(t)} = \bigl(p_1^{(t)},\dots,p_n^{(t)}\bigr)^\top$,  
      $p_i^{(t)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx_i^\top \vbeta^{(t)}}}$ predicted probabilities of success for data

    - $\mW^{(t)} = \diag\!\bigl(p_i^{(t)}(1-p_i^{(t)})\bigr)$ diagaonal matrix of regression weights

    - $\vz^{(t)} = \mX\vbeta^{(t)} + \bigl(\mW^{(t)}\bigr)^{-1}(\vy-\vp^{(t)})$, where $\vy-\vp^{(t)}$ is the vector of residuals

    - Update:
      $$
      \vbeta^{(t+1)}
      =
    \arg\min_{\vb}
    \sum_{i=1}^n
    w_i^{(t)}\bigl(z_i^{(t)} - \vx_i^\top \vb\bigr)^2
      =
      \left(\mX^\top \mW^{(t)} \mX\right)^{-1}
      \mX^\top \mW^{(t)} \vz^{(t)}
      $$


## Inference

- MLE: $\hvbeta = \arg\max_{\vb} \ell(\vb)$

- Approximate distribution: $\hvbeta \appxsim \Norm\!\bigl(\vbeta,\; (\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\bigr)$

- Weights: $\mW = \diag\bigl(p_i(1-p_i)\bigr)$

- Standard errors: $\SE(\hbeta_j) = \sqrt{\bigl[(\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}}$

- Wald statistic: $\displaystyle \frac{\hat\beta_j}{\text{SE}(\hat\beta_j)} \approx \Norm(0,1)$

- Likelihood ratio statistic for nested models: $2\bigl(\ell(\text{full})-\ell(\text{reduced})\bigr) \approx \chi^2_\nu$

- Same philosophy as OLS: test whether predictors matter

## Decision boundary (prediction)

- For a new $\vx$: $\hp(\vx) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\vx^\top \hvbeta}}$

- Classification: $\hY(\vx) = 1$ if $\hp(\vx) > 0.5$, else $0$

- Boundary: $\hp(\vx)=0.5 \iff \vx^\top \hvbeta = 0$

- Uncertainty in $\hp(\vx)$ comes from $\hvbeta$:
  $$
  \text{SE}\bigl(\hp(\vx)\bigr)
  \approx
  \hp(\vx)\bigl(1-\hp(\vx)\bigr)
  \sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top \mW \mX)^{-1}\vx}
  $$

- Confidence interval for probability:
  $\hp(\vx) \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\text{SE}\bigl(\hp(\vx)\bigr)$

- Classification confidence:
  - uncertain if $\hp(\vx) \approx 0.5$
  - more confident as $\hp(\vx)$ moves away from $0.5$


## Probit link

- Logit: $\logit(p) = \displaystyle \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ is the [quantile function]{.alert} of the logistic distribution

- Probit: $\probit(p) = \Phi^{-1}(p)$ is the [quantile function]{.alert} of the standard normal distribution
  - Model is $\probit(p(\vx)) = \vx^\top \vbeta$
  - $p(\vx) = \Phi(\vx^\top \vbeta)$, where $\Phi$ is the CDF of $\Norm(0,1)$

- Both:
  - map $(0,1) \to \reals$
  - give similar fits

- probit interpretation:
  - latent normal threshold model
  - lighter tails than logit


## Big picture

- Linear model models mean directly

- Logistic regression models transformed mean (log-odds)

- Key idea: keep linear structure, change the scale


## Generalized linear models

- Form: $g(\mu_i) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta$

- Components:
  - distribution of $Y_i$
  - linear predictor $\vx_i^\top \vbeta$
  - link $g$

---

## Examples

- OLS: $Y_i \sim \Norm(\mu_i,\sigma^2)$, $g(\mu)=\mu$

- Logistic: $Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)$, $g(p)=\log\frac{p}{1-p}$

- Poisson: $Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_i)$, $g(\mu)=\log \mu$

---

## Big idea

- $g(\Ex[Y_i]) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta$

- Same linear structure, different scale + distribution

## Generalized linear model

Three components:

1. Random component  
   $$
   Y_i \sim \text{distribution with mean } \mu_i
   $$

2. Systematic component  
   $$
   \eta_i = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
   $$

3. Link function  
   $$
   g(\mu_i) = \eta_i
   $$

---

## Unified form

$$
g(\Ex[Y_i]) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
$$

- linear model survives
- only the scale changes

---

## Examples

- OLS:
  $$
  Y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\sigma^2),
  \quad g(\mu)=\mu
  $$

- Logistic:
  $$
  Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i),
  \quad g(p)=\log\frac{p}{1-p}
  $$

---

## Another example (preview)

- Counts:
  $$
  Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_i)
  $$

- Link:
  $$
  \log \mu_i = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
  $$

- Ensures:
  - $\mu_i > 0$

---

## Why links?

- Match range:
  - probability $\in (0,1)$
  - counts $\ge 0$

- Make model linear:
  $$
  g(\mu_i) \text{ linear in } \vx_i
  $$

---

## Variance is built in

- OLS:
  $$
  \var(Y_i)=\sigma^2
  $$

- Logistic:
  $$
  \var(Y_i)=p_i(1-p_i)
  $$

- Poisson:
  $$
  \var(Y_i)=\mu_i
  $$

- Mean determines variance

---

## Estimation

- Likelihood:
  $$
  L(\vbeta) = \prod f(y_i \mid \mu_i)
  $$

- Solve:
  $$
  \nabla \ell(\vbeta)=0
  $$

- Numerical:
  - IRLS (generalizes least squares)

---

## Inference

- Same playbook:

  - Wald tests  
  - Likelihood ratio tests  

- Compare nested models

---

## Big picture

- Linear model:
  - structure stays

- GLM:
  - choose:
    - distribution
    - link

- Covers:
  - regression
  - ANOVA
  - classification
  - counts


## Same skeleton everywhere

$$
g(\mu_i) = \vx_i^\top \vbeta
$$

- OLS:
  $$
  g(\mu)=\mu
  $$

- Logistic:
  $$
  g(\mu)=\log\frac{\mu}{1-\mu}
  $$

- Poisson:
  $$
  g(\mu)=\log \mu
  $$

---

- Linear model stays  
- Only the scale changes
